stem: A 33-year-old G3P0 presents after her third consecutive first-trimester miscarriage. The first was a missed miscarriage at 9 weeks managed expectantly, the second was a complete miscarriage at 8 weeks managed at home, and the third was a missed miscarriage at 10+2 managed with misoprostol 4 weeks ago. She conceives easily within 1 to 2 cycles each time and has regular 28-day ovulatory cycles. She has no personal history of venous thromboembolism, deep vein thrombosis, stroke, or pulmonary embolism, no symptoms of systemic lupus erythematosus (no malar rash, no arthralgia, no oral ulcers, no Raynaud), no autoimmune disease, no fertility treatment, and no live births. She has never smoked, drinks one standard drink per week, has a BMI of 27, and is otherwise well. Her partner is a 35-year-old non-smoker with no children from previous relationships. There is no family history of recurrent miscarriage, balanced translocation, congenital malformation, or genetic disease, and no consanguinity. She is taking folic acid 0.5 mg daily. Examination is unremarkable. A transvaginal ultrasound shows a normal uterine cavity, both ovaries normal, and no fibroids; antral follicle count is normal. Karyotype of products of conception from the third miscarriage was 46,XX (euploid). TSH is 1.8 mIU/L and HbA1c is 5.4% (35 mmol/mol). She asks what should be tested before her next pregnancy.
lead_in: Which is the most important next investigation?

options.text: Parental peripheral blood karyotype testing as the very first single investigation
options.rationale: Parental karyotype is not routinely first-line; it is reserved for couples with a euploid first-trimester loss combined with additional features (advanced maternal age, family history) or when products show structural rearrangement. Yield of parental balanced translocation is approximately 2 to 5% (ESHRE RPL 2023; RANZCOG).

options.text: Inherited thrombophilia screen (factor V Leiden, prothrombin gene, protein C and S, antithrombin)
options.rationale: Inherited thrombophilia testing is not recommended in recurrent miscarriage outside of research settings because results do not change management of pregnancy loss; treatment with anticoagulation in inherited thrombophilia alone does not improve live birth rates (RANZCOG; ESHRE).

options.text: Diagnostic hysteroscopy regardless of any prior pelvic ultrasound findings reported
options.rationale: Saline infusion sonography or hysteroscopy is reserved for cases where ultrasound is equivocal or suggests cavity abnormality; her TVUS shows a normal cavity (ESHRE).

options.text: Lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin antibodies, and anti-beta-2-glycoprotein-1 antibodies on two occasions 12 weeks apart
options.rationale: After 3 consecutive miscarriages (or 2 by RANZCOG criteria), antiphospholipid syndrome screening is the highest yield test that has direct therapeutic implications (aspirin plus prophylactic LMWH in next pregnancy if positive); diagnosis requires laboratory criteria on two occasions at least 12 weeks apart (RANZCOG Recurrent Miscarriage; ESHRE RPL 2023).

options.text: Peripheral blood natural killer cell assay as the first investigation
options.rationale: Peripheral or uterine NK cell testing is not validated for recurrent miscarriage and is explicitly not recommended by ESHRE and RANZCOG; it does not change management and adds unjustified expense (ESHRE RPL 2023).

stem: A 30-year-old G0 and her 32-year-old male partner present after 13 months of trying to conceive with regular unprotected intercourse 2 to 3 times per week, including timed intercourse around predicted ovulation in the last 4 cycles. She has regular 29-day cycles with predictable ovulatory symptoms (mid-cycle mucus change and mittelschmerz). She has no dysmenorrhoea, no dyspareunia, no pelvic surgery, no PID, no STI history, and was up to date with cervical screening 6 months ago (HPV negative). BMI 24, non-smoker, no alcohol, no caffeine excess. She takes folic acid 0.5 mg daily and iodine 150 microg daily. Her partner is well, BMI 26, non-smoker, no known fertility issues, no previous children, no varicocele symptoms, no testicular trauma, no mumps orchitis, no inguinal surgery, no cryptorchidism, no chronic illness, no anabolic steroid use, takes no medications, drinks 6 standard drinks per week, and his work as an electrician does not involve heat exposure. There is no family history of infertility, premature menopause, or genetic disease on either side. On her examination there are no abnormal findings; bimanual is normal with no nodularity. She is concerned and asks what investigations should be done now.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial set of investigations?

options.text: Semen analysis, day-21 progesterone, TSH and transvaginal ultrasound; reserve HSG for after initial workup
options.rationale: Infertility is defined as 12 months of unprotected intercourse without conception in women under 35 (6 months if 35 or over). First-line investigations are semen analysis (assesses 40 to 50% of contributory causes), confirmation of ovulation (mid-luteal progesterone), thyroid function, and pelvic ultrasound. Tubal patency assessment (HSG or HyCoSy) is performed after initial workup if no abnormality is found (RANZCOG Infertility; NICE CG156).

options.text: Refer immediately for IVF without any further investigation of the couple
options.rationale: IVF is indicated after a defined cause is identified or following empirical workup; jumping to IVF without identifying treatable causes is inappropriate and ignores potential male factor (RANZCOG).

options.text: Continue trying for another 12 months before any investigation of the couple
options.rationale: She is over 12 months of regular unprotected intercourse and meets the definition of infertility; delaying investigation reduces her chances of timely treatment (RANZCOG; NICE CG156).

options.text: Hysterosalpingogram and diagnostic laparoscopy as the first-line investigations of the couple
options.rationale: Tubal patency assessment is part of the workup but follows initial investigations (semen analysis, ovulation confirmation, ultrasound); laparoscopy as first-line is invasive and unjustified (RANZCOG; NICE CG156).

options.text: AMH for ovarian reserve only and reassure her she can wait given young age
options.rationale: AMH is part of the broader workup but does not predict spontaneous conception; reassurance without male factor or tubal workup misses 50% of contributory causes (RANZCOG; ESHRE).

stem: A 15-year-old presents with her mother because of worsening period pain and bleeding. Menarche was at 12; cycles are regular at 28 days but have become increasingly painful since age 14, now causing her to miss 3 to 5 school days per month. Pain begins 24 hours before bleeding, peaks on day 2, and is associated with nausea, vomiting, and inability to get out of bed. She soaks through a fully saturated super pad every 90 minutes on days 1 and 2 and reports passing 5-cent-piece-sized clots. She has tried paracetamol 1 g qid and ibuprofen 400 mg tds with only partial relief. Her mother had endometriosis diagnosed at laparoscopy at age 22 and a paternal aunt has known endometriosis. There is no dyspareunia (she is not sexually active), no dyschezia, no haematuria, no fever, and no postcoital bleeding. She has no chronic disease. She is no longer attending netball training and has begun to refuse social outings and sleepovers during her periods, and her mother reports she has been increasingly tearful and withdrawn in the days leading up to bleeding. On abdominal examination there is mild suprapubic tenderness; she declines an internal examination. A pregnancy test is negative.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Naproxen 500 mg bd (day -1 to 3), continuous COCP and pelvic ultrasound; refer if no improvement
options.rationale: Adolescent dysmenorrhoea with severe functional impact, heavy bleeding, family history of endometriosis, and inadequate response to first-line analgesia warrants empirical scheduled NSAID plus continuous (cycle-suppressing) COCP, transabdominal pelvic ultrasound (transvaginal not appropriate in a non-sexually-active adolescent), and specialist referral if no improvement at 3 months; diagnosis does not require laparoscopy in adolescents (RANZCOG Endometriosis; Pelvic Pain Foundation Australia; ESHRE).

options.text: Reassure her that severe period pain in adolescence is normal and continue her current analgesia regimen long-term
options.rationale: Missing 3 to 5 school days per month, social withdrawal, and inadequate response to first-line analgesia is not normal and reflects a high probability of endometriosis or other secondary cause; dismissing severe dysmenorrhoea delays diagnosis by an average of 7 to 12 years (Pelvic Pain Foundation Australia; RANZCOG).

options.text: Proceed directly to diagnostic laparoscopy without trialling any medical management as the first step
options.rationale: Laparoscopy is not first-line in adolescents; empirical medical therapy is trialled first and laparoscopy reserved for those who fail medical management or have suspected deep disease (RANZCOG; ESHRE).

options.text: Commence a GnRH agonist with hormone add-back therapy as the next immediate step in management
options.rationale: GnRH agonists are reserved for refractory endometriosis after failure of first-line hormonal therapy; long-term use in adolescents carries bone density concerns and is not first-line (ESHRE).

options.text: Insert a 52 mg levonorgestrel intrauterine system without trialling any prior medical therapy first
options.rationale: LNG-IUS is effective for adolescent endometriosis and heavy menstrual bleeding but is generally a second-line option after COCP trial in a non-sexually-active adolescent; insertion may require general anaesthetic (Family Planning Australia; RANZCOG).

stem: A 14-year-old presents to the emergency department with a 4-hour history of sudden severe left iliac fossa pain. The pain began abruptly while she was playing netball, is sharp, constant, radiates to the left flank, and has been associated with vomiting four times in the last 2 hours. She had two previous brief episodes of less severe left-sided pain in the last 6 months, each lasting 30 minutes and resolving spontaneously. Menarche was at 13; her last menstrual period was 12 days ago and was normal. She has never been sexually active. There is no fever, no urinary symptoms, no diarrhoea, no PR bleeding, no recent trauma, and no chronic illness. On examination she is in obvious distress, BP 110/68, HR 116, T 37.0, with marked left iliac fossa tenderness and guarding, and a tender palpable mass. Abdomen is otherwise soft. Beta-hCG is negative. White cell count is 13.4 x 10^9/L, CRP 18 mg/L. A transabdominal pelvic ultrasound with Doppler shows a 7 cm complex left adnexal mass with peripherally displaced follicles, a hyperechoic whirlpool sign at the suspected pedicle, and absent venous flow with reduced arterial flow within the left ovary. The right ovary is normal. There is no free fluid. She has fasted for 5 hours, has IV access in the right antecubital fossa, and morphine 5 mg IV has been given with partial relief. Her parents have given consent for emergency anaesthesia and laparoscopy. The gynaecology registrar and on-call anaesthetist have reviewed her and the operating theatre is available within 30 minutes.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Admit for observation and analgesia and repeat the ultrasound in 12 hours
options.rationale: Ovarian torsion is a time-critical surgical emergency; every hour of delay increases ovarian necrosis and reduces salvage rate. Observation while imaging declares itself is harmful (RANZCOG; KEMH).

options.text: Pelvic MRI to confirm diagnosis before any surgical decision is made
options.rationale: MRI adds delay without improving on Doppler ultrasound findings that are already characteristic; the diagnosis is a clinical and ultrasound diagnosis warranting immediate surgery (RANZCOG).

options.text: Emergency laparoscopy for detorsion and ovarian preservation, with oophoropexy considered intraoperatively
options.rationale: Whirlpool sign, peripherally displaced follicles and absent venous flow on Doppler in an adolescent with characteristic clinical features is ovarian torsion. The standard of care is emergency laparoscopy with detorsion regardless of macroscopic appearance (ovaries that look black often recover function); oophorectomy is reserved for necrotic non-viable tissue or malignancy. Oophoropexy is considered to reduce recurrence (RANZCOG Ovarian Cysts; ESHRE; Royal Women's Hospital Clinical Guidelines).

options.text: Emergency laparoscopy with planned left salpingo-oophorectomy because the ovary appears non-viable on Doppler
options.rationale: Doppler signs do not reliably predict viability; even ovaries that appear black at surgery have a high rate of functional recovery after detorsion. Oophorectomy in an adolescent should be reserved for confirmed necrosis or malignancy (RANZCOG; Royal Women's Hospital).

options.text: Commence broad-spectrum IV antibiotics and admit for suspected pelvic inflammatory disease
options.rationale: She has never been sexually active and the imaging is diagnostic of torsion, not tubo-ovarian abscess; antibiotics do not treat torsion and delay surgery (Australian STI Management Guidelines; RANZCOG).

stem: A 29-year-old G0 woman with PCOS diagnosed 18 months ago (Rotterdam criteria: oligomenorrhoea, biochemical hyperandrogenism with elevated free androgen index, polycystic ovarian morphology with 26 follicles per ovary) has been trying to conceive for 12 months with intercourse 2 to 3 times per week. Her partner has a normal semen analysis on two occasions. She has bilateral patent tubes on HyCoSy. AMH 38 pmol/L, AFC 28, normal TSH and prolactin. She has lost 6 kg over 6 months with structured lifestyle intervention (current BMI 28, down from 31), and her cycles remain oligomenorrhoeic (45 to 70 days). HbA1c 5.3% and OGTT normal. She has no contraindications to ovulation induction agents and is keen to commence pharmacological treatment. She is taking folic acid 0.5 mg daily. Pelvic ultrasound today shows polycystic-appearing ovaries with no cysts or other pathology. She has no other comorbidities. She asks which agent is recommended as first-line ovulation induction in PCOS. She has no other endocrine or metabolic comorbidity, no contraindication to letrozole or clomiphene, and she has had a pre-treatment FSH and oestradiol confirmed within normal range.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological agent for ovulation induction in this patient?

options.text: Letrozole 2.5 mg daily for 5 days from cycle day 2 to 6, titrated upward if anovulatory
options.rationale: The 2023 international PCOS guideline and ASRM both recommend letrozole as first-line ovulation induction in PCOS for anovulatory infertility; letrozole achieves higher ovulation, pregnancy and live birth rates than clomiphene with lower multiple pregnancy risk (2023 International PCOS Guideline; ASRM; RANZCOG).

options.text: Clomiphene citrate 50 mg daily for 5 days from cycle day 2 to 6 with serial follicular tracking
options.rationale: Clomiphene was historically first-line but the PPCOS II trial and subsequent meta-analyses show letrozole achieves higher live birth rates with lower multiple pregnancy risk; clomiphene is now considered second-line or used if letrozole is unavailable (2023 International PCOS Guideline; ASRM).

options.text: Metformin 500 mg twice daily titrated to 1 g twice daily as sole agent for ovulation induction
options.rationale: Metformin as monotherapy has lower ovulation, pregnancy and live birth rates than letrozole; it may be added to letrozole or clomiphene as adjunct (particularly with BMI >25 or insulin resistance) but is not first-line monotherapy for ovulation induction (2023 International PCOS Guideline; ASRM).

options.text: Recombinant FSH gonadotrophin injection from cycle day 2 with daily follicular ultrasound monitoring
options.rationale: Injectable gonadotrophins are second-line if oral ovulation induction fails; they carry higher cost, OHSS risk and multiple-pregnancy risk, and require intensive monitoring (2023 International PCOS Guideline; RANZCOG).

options.text: Laparoscopic ovarian drilling under general anaesthesia as a first-line surgical option for her
options.rationale: Laparoscopic ovarian drilling is a second-line option for women with clomiphene/letrozole resistance, particularly when other indications for laparoscopy exist; it is not first-line for treatment-naive PCOS (2023 International PCOS Guideline; RANZCOG).

stem: A 32-year-old woman is referred to endocrinology with 14 months of secondary amenorrhoea, bilateral galactorrhoea, reduced libido and a 4-month history of headaches behind her eyes worse on waking. Two months ago she noticed difficulty seeing the side of her vision when driving. On examination today she has bitemporal hemianopia confirmed on confrontation and Humphrey perimetry. Prolactin is 9800 mIU/L (confirmed on a repeat fasting morning sample), TSH and free T4 are normal, beta-hCG negative, renal function normal and she takes no dopamine-blocking medications, antipsychotics, antidepressants or opioids. MRI pituitary demonstrates a 14 mm pituitary mass with suprasellar extension contacting the optic chiasm with no cavernous sinus invasion. Other anterior pituitary hormones (ACTH/cortisol axis, IGF-1, TSH/T4, LH/FSH) are otherwise within reference range. She has no contraindications to dopamine agonist therapy, no history of impulse control disorder and no cardiac valve disease. She is keen to preserve future fertility. She is highly motivated to preserve fertility, has a partner and is keen to avoid surgery if possible. She has no symptoms of pituitary apoplexy (no thunderclap headache, no collapse, no cranial nerve palsy) and her visual loss has been gradual over months rather than days.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Cabergoline 0.25 mg twice weekly initially with close ophthalmology and endocrine follow-up
options.rationale: Dopamine agonists (cabergoline preferred over bromocriptine for efficacy and tolerability) are first-line for macroprolactinomas including those with visual field deficits; tumour shrinkage and visual recovery occur in the majority within weeks to months. Surgery is reserved for failure of medical therapy or apoplexy (Endocrine Society Hyperprolactinaemia 2011; eTG: Endocrinology).

options.text: Urgent transsphenoidal surgical resection of the pituitary mass within 48 hours
options.rationale: Transsphenoidal surgery is reserved for failed medical therapy, dopamine agonist intolerance, pituitary apoplexy or rapidly progressive visual loss not responsive to dopamine agonist; first-line for prolactinoma even with chiasmal compression is medical therapy (Endocrine Society Hyperprolactinaemia 2011).

options.text: External beam radiotherapy to the pituitary fossa as primary management
options.rationale: Radiotherapy is third-line, used after failed surgery or for residual aggressive disease; it has slow effect and risks hypopituitarism, so it is not first-line for a treatment-naive prolactinoma (Endocrine Society Hyperprolactinaemia 2011).

options.text: Observation with serial MRI and visual fields, deferring treatment until prolactin >20,000 mIU/L
options.rationale: Observation is inappropriate in the presence of a macroadenoma with visual field deficit; untreated chiasmal compression risks permanent visual loss, and dopamine agonist therapy is highly effective (Endocrine Society Hyperprolactinaemia 2011).

options.text: Cyclical combined oral contraceptive pill alone to restore menses and protect bone density
options.rationale: COCP treats secondary amenorrhoea symptomatically but does not address the tumour or visual field compromise; it can mask prolactin response monitoring and is not first-line (Endocrine Society Hyperprolactinaemia 2011).

stem: A 28-year-old G0 woman with PCOS (AMH 48 pmol/L pre-stimulation) presents to the emergency department 9 days after oocyte retrieval (28 oocytes collected, fresh embryo transferred). She reports 3 days of progressive abdominal distension, breathlessness on minimal exertion and reduced urine output. On examination she is tachycardic (HR 112), BP 102/64, RR 24, SpO2 94% on room air, T 36.9. Abdomen is markedly distended with tense ascites and reduced bowel sounds; she has bilateral basal crackles on chest auscultation. Bloods: haematocrit 0.51, WCC 22 x10^9/L, sodium 131 mmol/L, potassium 5.0 mmol/L, creatinine 124 micromol/L (baseline 60), urea 9.5 mmol/L, albumin 26 g/L, ALT 75 U/L, AST 68 U/L. CXR shows small bilateral pleural effusions. Pelvic ultrasound shows ovaries 12 cm bilaterally with large volume ascites extending above the umbilicus. Urine output has been 18 mL/hour over the last 4 hours. She has no chest pain or calf swelling but D-dimer is markedly elevated. She has no abdominal peritonism on examination and a bedside ECG shows sinus tachycardia without ischaemic changes. She has been counselled that severe OHSS is a recognised complication of high-response IVF.
lead_in: Which combination of interventions is the most appropriate immediate management priority?

options.text: Admit; commence IV crystalloid with albumin, prophylactic enoxaparin, fluid balance and urgent paracentesis
options.rationale: Severe/critical OHSS with haemoconcentration (Hct >0.50), WCC >20, oliguria, tense ascites and respiratory compromise requires admission, IV crystalloid (with albumin in severe hypoalbuminaemia), VTE prophylaxis (high independent VTE risk in OHSS), strict fluid balance and paracentesis to relieve tense ascites/respiratory compromise/renal dysfunction (RANZCOG OHSS; ESHRE OHSS).

options.text: Discharge home with oral fluids, simple analgesia and outpatient review in 48 hours
options.rationale: Severe OHSS with haemoconcentration, oliguria, tense ascites and respiratory compromise mandates admission and IV therapy; outpatient management is inappropriate at this severity (RANZCOG OHSS; ESHRE OHSS).

options.text: Admit and commence intravenous frusemide infusion to address oliguria and reduce the ovarian volume
options.rationale: Diuretics are contraindicated in OHSS until intravascular volume is restored; they worsen haemoconcentration and renal dysfunction in a third-space sequestration state (RANZCOG OHSS; ESHRE OHSS).

options.text: Urgent laparotomy with bilateral oophorectomy to remove the hyperstimulated ovaries definitively
options.rationale: Surgery in OHSS is reserved for complications such as ovarian torsion, rupture with haemorrhage or ectopic pregnancy; hyperstimulated ovaries are friable and surgery risks bleeding and oophorectomy (RANZCOG OHSS).

options.text: Commence broad-spectrum IV antibiotics and antiviral therapy pending blood culture results
options.rationale: Elevated WCC in OHSS is part of the inflammatory state; empirical antibiotics are not indicated unless infection is clinically suspected. Antibiotic stewardship and source-directed treatment apply (RANZCOG OHSS).

stem: A 34-year-old G2P2 woman attends her GP for contraceptive review. She was diagnosed 14 months ago with stage II oestrogen-receptor-positive, progesterone-receptor-positive, HER2-negative invasive ductal carcinoma of the right breast. She had wide local excision plus axillary node sampling (1 of 3 nodes positive), adjuvant chemotherapy and radiotherapy, and commenced tamoxifen 20 mg daily 6 months ago which she plans to continue for at least 5 to 10 years. She has been amenorrhoeic since chemotherapy but recent bloods show a partial return of menses and an FSH of 18 IU/L with oestradiol 280 pmol/L, suggesting recovery of ovarian function. She uses condoms intermittently with her long-term male partner. She has completed her family. She is anxious to avoid pregnancy because she does not wish to interrupt her endocrine therapy and is aware of the teratogenicity of tamoxifen. She has no other major comorbidity. BMI 24, BP 118/74. The breast oncology team has documented that she should avoid any exogenous oestrogen for the duration of tamoxifen and ideally for any further endocrine therapy. She has no history of menorrhagia, no current irregular bleeding and no contraindication to a copper intrauterine device.
lead_in: Which contraceptive method is most appropriate for this woman?

options.text: Copper intrauterine device (Cu-IUD) inserted at the next available family planning visit
options.rationale: Current and past breast cancer is UKMEC 4 for ALL hormonal contraception (combined and progestogen-only). The copper IUD is UKMEC 1 and is the recommended highly effective reversible option in a woman with breast cancer who has completed her family; female sterilisation is also acceptable (FSRH UKMEC 2016; RANZCOG: Contraception).

options.text: Levonorgestrel intrauterine system (Mirena) inserted at the next available appointment
options.rationale: Current breast cancer is UKMEC 4 for the LNG-IUS; past breast cancer (>5 years remission) is UKMEC 3. Despite local action, systemic progestogen exposure means the LNG-IUS is NOT recommended in current or recent breast cancer (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill at the lowest effective oestrogen dose available
options.rationale: Current or recent breast cancer is UKMEC 4 for combined hormonal contraception due to the oestrogen-receptor-positive nature of the disease and the high recurrence risk; the COCP is absolutely contraindicated (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

options.text: Etonogestrel subdermal contraceptive implant (Implanon NXT) for 3 years
options.rationale: Current breast cancer is UKMEC 4 for the progestogen-only implant; all hormonal methods are contraindicated and progestogen-only methods do not avoid the UKMEC 4 rating in current or recent breast cancer (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

options.text: Depot medroxyprogesterone acetate 150 mg IM every 12 weeks
options.rationale: DMPA is UKMEC 4 in current breast cancer; all hormonal methods are contraindicated. The copper IUD is the recommended highly effective reversible option (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

stem: A 31-year-old G1P1 woman with systemic lupus erythematosus (diagnosed 6 years ago) attends for contraceptive review. Her disease has been clinically and serologically stable for 18 months on hydroxychloroquine and low-dose prednisolone (5 mg daily). She has previously had two episodes of cutaneous lupus and one episode of mild lupus nephritis treated with mycophenolate, now in remission. Her most recent immunology shows positive lupus anticoagulant on two occasions 12 weeks apart, anticardiolipin IgG strongly positive, and anti-beta-2-glycoprotein-I positive (triple positive antiphospholipid profile). She has no personal history of venous thromboembolism, stroke or ischaemic heart disease and no obstetric morbidity but is high risk for VTE. BP 120/76, BMI 25, non-smoker. She has completed her family, uses condoms inconsistently and would like a more reliable method. She is concerned about both medication interactions with hydroxychloroquine and the impact of contraception on her lupus and VTE risk. Her rheumatology team has documented that combined oestrogen-containing contraception is absolutely contraindicated in the setting of triple-positive antiphospholipid syndrome. She has no contraindication to an intrauterine system and has had a normal recent cervical screen and pelvic examination.
lead_in: Which contraceptive method is most appropriate for this woman?

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill with the lowest available oestrogen dose to minimise VTE risk
options.rationale: SLE with positive antiphospholipid antibodies (or unknown status) is UKMEC 4 for combined hormonal contraception due to substantially elevated VTE and arterial thrombosis risk; the COCP is absolutely contraindicated (FSRH UKMEC 2016; EULAR Lupus).

options.text: Levonorgestrel intrauterine system (LNG-IUS) inserted at the next available appointment
options.rationale: In SLE with positive antiphospholipid antibodies, the LNG-IUS is UKMEC 1 to 2: highly effective, no systemic oestrogen, minimal interaction with hydroxychloroquine, low VTE risk, and acceptable in a woman who has completed her family. The Cu-IUD (UKMEC 1) is also acceptable but the LNG-IUS additionally reduces menstrual blood loss (relevant if anticoagulation is initiated later) (FSRH UKMEC 2016; EULAR Lupus).

options.text: Combined hormonal contraceptive vaginal ring as it avoids first-pass hepatic metabolism
options.rationale: All combined hormonal methods (pill, patch, ring) share the same UKMEC 4 rating in SLE with positive antiphospholipid antibodies; route does not modify the thrombotic risk (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

options.text: Depot medroxyprogesterone acetate 150 mg IM every 12 weeks for ongoing contraception
options.rationale: DMPA is UKMEC 3 in SLE with positive antiphospholipid antibodies because of its adverse lipid and small but increased VTE association; an IUD (LNG or Cu) is preferred (FSRH UKMEC 2016; EULAR Lupus).

options.text: Continue barrier contraception alone (condoms) given the complexity of medication interactions
options.rationale: Condoms have high typical-use failure rates (~13% per year) and are inadequate for a woman who has completed her family and seeks reliable contraception; effective LARC is available and appropriate (FSRH UKMEC 2016; RANZCOG: Contraception).

stem: A 31-year-old G2P2 woman attends for contraceptive review 7 months after Roux-en-Y gastric bypass surgery. She has lost 28 kg (BMI now 32, down from 42 pre-operatively). She has not yet resumed menses since surgery and reports breast tenderness, fatigue and intermittent nausea, which she attributes to her vitamin and mineral supplementation. A urine beta-hCG today is negative. Her past medical history includes well-controlled hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily (BP today 128/78) and previous gestational diabetes (no current diabetes; HbA1c 5.5%). She uses condoms inconsistently with her long-term male partner. Pre-operatively she was on the combined oral contraceptive pill which was stopped 6 weeks before surgery as part of the VTE risk minimisation protocol. She is fully aware that bariatric surgery is associated with rapid return of fertility and that pregnancy is recommended to be deferred for 12 to 24 months post-operatively. She wishes to use a highly effective method until she is ready for a third pregnancy in 18 months. She has been advised that bariatric surgery, particularly Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, results in malabsorption of oral hormonal contraception and reduces the reliability of the combined oral contraceptive pill and the progestogen-only pill. The bariatric service has also recommended deferring pregnancy for 12 to 24 months to allow weight stabilisation, to minimise rapid weight regain and to permit safe titration of micronutrient supplementation including thiamine, B12 and iron prior to conception.
lead_in: Which contraceptive method is most appropriate for this woman?

options.text: Levonorgestrel intrauterine system (Mirena), copper IUD or etonogestrel subdermal implant
options.rationale: After malabsorptive bariatric procedures (Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, biliopancreatic diversion), oral hormonal contraceptive absorption is unreliable; LARCs that bypass the gut (LNG-IUS, Cu-IUD, etonogestrel implant) are preferred. The COCP also carries elevated VTE risk in the post-operative period and may be UKMEC 3 to 4 with persistent obesity and other risk factors; pregnancy should be delayed 12 to 24 months post-bariatric surgery (FSRH UKMEC 2016; ASMBS/ACOG; RANZCOG).

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill at standard dose with continued condom backup
options.rationale: Roux-en-Y gastric bypass impairs absorption of oral hormonal contraception, reducing efficacy; FSRH advises against COCP and POP after malabsorptive bariatric surgery (FSRH UKMEC 2016; ASMBS/ACOG Bariatric Pregnancy).

options.text: Progestogen-only pill taken daily with a 3-hour mistiming window
options.rationale: Like the COCP, the POP has reduced and variable absorption after Roux-en-Y gastric bypass; non-oral methods are preferred (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

options.text: Fertility awareness method timed to predicted ovulation each cycle
options.rationale: Fertility awareness has high typical-use failure rates (~24% per year) and is particularly unreliable in the post-bariatric period when menses are returning irregularly; it does not meet the brief for highly effective contraception (Therapeutic Guidelines: Contraception).

options.text: Recommend immediate pregnancy attempts given strong weight loss and improving metabolic profile
options.rationale: Current expert consensus (ASMBS/ACOG, RANZCOG) recommends avoiding pregnancy for 12 to 24 months after bariatric surgery to allow weight stabilisation, optimise nutrition (B12, iron, folate, vitamin D) and reduce the risk of SGA, prematurity and micronutrient deficiency in the foetus (ASMBS/ACOG Bariatric Pregnancy; RANZCOG).
